description: Ending a scope tears its members down in reverse creation order (#lzspecedgeindex). Graph state is order-independent — `disposeAll_order_independent` in lazily-formal proves teardown reaches the same graph whatever order the scope recorded its members — but effect **cleanups are side effects**, and their relative order is observable to the caller. A scope that acquires A then B then C must release C, B, A, because a later member may have been created against state an earlier one owns. This fixture uses THREE effects deliberately. The existing `scope_teardown_equals_fold_of_disposals.json` carries a `cleanup_order` assertion but its scope owns exactly one effect, and a runner that projects `cleanup_order` onto effect entries (which is what a binding whose derived slots have no cleanup callback will do) then sees a single-entry log that reads identically forwards and backwards. That fixture therefore cannot discriminate teardown order, and lazily-dart confirmed it by mutation: reversing the iteration left all nine reactive-graph fixtures green. Three effects make the sequence unambiguous under either projection — forward teardown yields [e_first, e_second, e_third] and cannot be mistaken for the required order. Nothing here asserts on graph state, only on the cleanup sequence, so a binding remains free to implement scope teardown as a bulk sweep rather than a per-member loop.

expect.note: all three effects are owned by the scope and have run once, so each has a cleanup outstanding
the discriminating assertion. Forward teardown yields [e_first, e_second, e_third]; a single-effect scope would yield the same one-entry log either way, which is why this one owns three.
every member is torn down, so a write to the surviving source reaches nothing — teardown detached the edges rather than merely running the cleanups
